claim: Terminus (tmux-only agent) achieves top-tier TerminalBench performance with minimal interface (only keystrokes + VT100 codes), proving most coding agent features may be unnecessary
actionable: When designing coding agents, start with minimal interface (bash + file operations); don't assume feature-rich UX is required for model performance.
source_title: I Hated Every Coding Agent, So I Built My Own — Mario Zechner (Pi)

claim: Cloud Code's feature bloat and hidden context injection create unpredictable workflows; terminal flicker and constant UI changes destabilize production usage patterns
actionable: Avoid tools with hidden context management or frequent UI changes; prioritize stability and observability over feature count in production coding agents.
source_title: I Hated Every Coding Agent, So I Built My Own — Mario Zechner (Pi)

claim: Open Code's LSP integration (real-time error feedback) harms agent performance by telling model intermediate edits are wrong before task completion
actionable: Only run linting/type-checking at natural synchronization points (when agent claims task is complete), not after every edit, to avoid confusing the model.
source_title: I Hated Every Coding Agent, So I Built My Own — Mario Zechner (Pi)

claim: Pi coding agent uses 4-tool minimal core (read/write/edit/bash) with hot-reloadable TypeScript extensions allowing custom compaction, permission gates, and remote execution
actionable: Design extensible coding agents with pluggable tools; allow users to override core tools (bash, file ops) and add custom context management (compaction, permission gates) via extensions.
source_title: I Hated Every Coding Agent, So I Built My Own — Mario Zechner (Pi)

claim: System prompt for coding agent should be minimal (single short paragraph); frontier models are already RL-trained to understand coding task context
actionable: Write concise system prompts for coding agents (under 100 tokens); avoid repeating 'you are a coding assistant' when models already know their role.
source_title: I Hated Every Coding Agent, So I Built My Own — Mario Zechner (Pi)

claim: Sub-agents reduce observability; use tmux sessions instead so you retain full visibility over agent context and tool calls
actionable: Instead of opaque sub-agents, spawn new agent instances in tmux sessions; this preserves observability and lets you inspect all context and decisions.
source_title: I Hated Every Coding Agent, So I Built My Own — Mario Zechner (Pi)

claim: Approval-based safety (human confirms each action) causes decision fatigue; better approach is containerization or write-only sandboxed environments
actionable: Don't rely on approval dialogs for agent safety; instead use process-level isolation (containers) or restrict agent capabilities (read-only, sandboxed writes).
source_title: I Hated Every Coding Agent, So I Built My Own — Mario Zechner (Pi)
